One of them is the infamous two siblings that I talk about everyday. The first of these is what I call an “elevator down” sell. This refers to selloffs where price plunges for a few minutes to a few hours with 0 supports...

Therefore, it would be time for chop, and all week, the range was mostly 6769 to 6815. On a normal day my lean is that on Sunday after the open ES put in a monster Failed Breakdown when we flushed the 6689 shelf of lows that we set last Friday, recovered, and ripped. My lean is this can keep going. Bulls want ...

The task today would be to try and clear this range out. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “We remain in a tight range in ES after Monday’s 200+ point rally. Support is mostly 6769 and resistance is 6815.” Today, ES finally broke the range and it was to the downside. As a result, sells triggered. Can bulls get back inside it?

article_title: SPX Breaks Down, But Is It Another Bear Trap? March 13 Plan
snippet: We closed up in the middle of some intense congestion around 6769 so there was little to do immediately other than hold our runners from yesterday’s failed breakdowns and wait for the elevator down sell.

prose_context.text: The first possible entry down was the 6750 (yesterday’s daily low) Failed Breakdown. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “Below 6758 is 6738-42. Remember my core rule in ES: No knife catching allowed. If ES is collapsing full speed into 6742-38 Never buy. If its a slow, controlled sell into the zone one can try but if we are freefalling it is much safer to wait fo ...
Trade Recap/Daily Summary

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At ~6750 we had #1. This was the low of day for Wednesday and the flush and recovery of this would be highly actionable IF we even got it.

By 7:30PM we hit 6716. ES spent some time sitting here (for a full hour basically). I don’t directly bid supports (only Failed Breakdowns) but for those who like these this level offered a good 20 points.

Specifically at 7:30PM ES set a significant low at 6716. This was a clear low from which we bounced 20 points, meaning the flush and recovery of that low would be actionable.

However, readers know that ES rarely travels in a straight line for long. What it does is it goes elevator down in a vertical in a vertical flush cutting through every support. Then, it flushes a big low, traps shorts, recovers, and price squeezes. This is my core setup: The Failed Breakdown. We saw this on Friday and we saw it again today.

On Friday however, this range finally broke down. This 6782 support was big support on Wednesday and Thursday last week. I wrote on Thursday at 4pm that: “Bear case Tomorrow: Begins below 6782.” Friday morning, we lost it, and down we went.

This week (until Friday) was a classic day traders market as ES is stuck trading in a large range which has been mostly 6782 to 6873 . By selling to 6715.75, ES lost Tuesday’s 6718.75 daily low. Shortly after, it recovered (Failed Breakdown) and the parabolic rip began to 6778-82 where we peaked Friday afternoon.

1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

2) The basic theme heading into today was that on Friday ES broke down the range it was stuck in for much last week, and this range had support at 6782. This is where ES set the low of day on Tuesday, and Wednesday. This 6782 level therefore became a bull/bear line with bulls controlling above, bears below. On Friday at 4pm, I expanded on the general cont...

Bull case Monday: Very simple. 6782 was a big support level on Wednesday and on Thursday setting lows there both days. Today, we lost it, and in order for bulls to regain control, they must recover it. Its held as resistance all day today. The only small plus bulls have now is that 9:50AM today ES put in a big Failed Breakdown of Tuesday’s 6718 low, but t...

Futures on Sunday opened with a major gap down (typical the last few weeks), with ES gapping down into 6692 then going elevator down into 6612 by 6pm. As readers know when ES is going elevator down, we never long and we wait for Failed Breakdowns. I wrote on Friday at 4pm: “rememeber my core rule in ES: No knife catching allowed. Most the time when ES sel...
Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At ~6680 on November 24th we set a massive low at 6680 from which we rallied for months. Its the exact kind of low we’d want to trade the recovery of IF and when it triggered. It did not unfortunately.

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

Remember though we cannot just rush into a Failed Breakdown. The golden rule is still the same: If you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low or Level, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and...

article_title: Bulls Bought The Dip With Force. Dead Cat Bounce, or Bottom? March 10th Plan
snippet: What happens after a big support shelf fails? Firstly we sell hard (elevator down). Secondly, we see a Failed Breakdown (6612 Failed Breakdown last evening). Thirdly, we typically back-test the broken down shelf. If bulls can recover the broken down shelf, it is a significant technical win.

These cycles prey on retail psychology and allow institutions to seek the liquidity they need to fill their orders in predictable ways. Once you understand these cycles, you can exploit them as well. One of those cycles is ES goes elevator down in a vertical flush cutting through every support. Then, it flushes a big low, traps shorts, recovers, and price...

Like every elevator down sell though, it would resolve with a short squeeze. Around 6:15AM Es set a nice 58 point low at 6742. We swept it down to 6717 this morning as posted. By 11am we recovered it. I live-tweeted 6742 reclaim starts us higher, and we were +100 in no time back inside the old range.

snippet: Heading into today, I was still holding my 10% long runner from the 6791 major Failed Breakdown we saw yesterday (since it occurred overnight, I couldn’t enter until 6808 at 7:30AM Monday morning). I did a deep dive into the long in yesterday’s trade recap section and it was provided in advance. I verified this positioning at the close yesterday when I wr...

prose_context.text: 1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

Like every elevator down sell though, it would resolve with a short squeeze. Around 6:15AM Es set a nice 58 point low at 6742. We swept it down to 6717 this morning as posted. By 11am we recovered it. I live-tweeted 6742 reclaim starts us higher, and we were +100 in no time back inside the old range.

We saw precisely this to open up this week. Over the weekend Iran headlines hit and ES went gap down and elevator down off the open from 6880s down to 6811 6pm Sunday. We bounced 27 points from there. At 7:40PM Sunday ES then trapped below that 6811 opening low down to 6809, recovered it shortly after (Failed Breakdown) and ripped to 6850-55 by 9pm.

article_title: Another Big Dip In SPX That Was Again Bought. Was That The Bottom? March 4 Plan
snippet: Monday Evening, The Start of the Sell, and the Possible 6853 Failed Breakdown

prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary

After a big rally I sit back, do nothing, and wait for lots of deep red to appear - readers know my trading is simple. I trade only after an elevator down sell. I don’t trade chop, I don’t trade indecision. I trade fast, deep red. We finally got this elevator down into 9pm after lots of chop around 6873 support.
Why is 6853 a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At ~6853 we had #2. At 11:30AM yesterday we set a huge low there, from which we rallied to 6912 high of day. This is a big low, the sort of which we’d want to see a flush and recovery of.

chart_matches.source_label: Monday Evening, The Start of the Sell, and the Possible 6853 Failed Breakdown We closed up yesterday after a monster Failed Breakdown and all-day rally.

article_title: Another Big Dip In SPX That Was Again Bought. Was That The Bottom? March 4 Plan
snippet: Unfortunately, we never got a long trigger on this 6853 Failed Breakdown. ES spent between 9pm and 10:45PM simply backtesting 6853 from below, rejecting, and never recovered. It is worth noting though this was a fantastic example of the first type of acceptance shown above.

prose_context.text: Remember the first type of acceptance occurs when back-tests the significant low from below, sells off, then returns to it. By doing this (selling off at the significant low then rebounding back to it), price tells us there is no supply at the significant low and we can safetly long above. Acceptance however is not a long trigger as readers know, nor is i...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

Here at 6768 we had the prior days low at 6768 and this was a massive low set on Monday from which we rallied over 100 points. Its flush and recovery would be actionable.

prose_context.text: A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. Here at 6768 we had the prior days low at 6768 and this was a massive low set on Monday from which we rallied over 100 points. Its flush and recovery would be actionable.
This was an absolutely textbook Failed Breakdown setup. Remember Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

Second, I noticed we got another quality Failed Breakdown overnight (and since we sold below by 6808ish entry from yesterday, my 10% lotto runner stopped out). I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “Nothing under there until 6759 but instead of buying it wait for it to hold and recover today’s 6768 daily low for a huge Failed Breakdown.”

When I woke up and checked price at 7:30AM, I noticed two things happened overnight. First, the bear case triggered, putting bears in control for today. Remember bears being in control does not mean there are now rallies - it just means we can anticipate they will mostly be sold after. prose_context.text: Remember my core rule in ES: No knife catching allowed in ES. Most the time when ES sells off it goes elevator down and when this is happening ES does not care about supports until the sell algo turns off (usually takes a few minutes to a few hours).
I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “Nothing under there until 6759 but instead of buying it wait for it to hold and recover today’s 6768 daily low for a huge Failed Breakdown.”

I wasn’t at the screen here and we were up at 6800 by the time I originally checked price (just below that big 6808 level) so I was good waiting for that to recover which I also tweeted at 8am. It never did and we headed lower into 8:50am down to 6759 support.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. Here at 6768 we had the prior days low at 6768 and this was a massive low set on Monday from which we rallied over 100 points. Its flush and recovery would be actionable.

article_title: SPX Is Again Coiled Tightly. Move Incoming. What Way? Feb 20th Plan
snippet: Wednesday Evening and the Thursday 6:20AM 6872 Failed Breakdown:

prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary

The first obvious Failed Breakdown was the Failed Breakdown of the 6872 3:15PM low (assuming we even got it as a trigger). I provided this setup yesterday at 4pm when I wrote: “The first proper support down is 6877. We held this late day today at 3:15PM more or less. It is well tested now. While one could try giving it another bid, remember my core rule: ...
Why 6872? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At ~6872 we had #2. At 3:15PM on Wednesday, ES set a huge low there from which we rallied 30 points. This is a clear, V-shaped low of the sort we’d want to see a flush and recovery of.

article_title: My Trade Methodology - Fundamentals
snippet: 9) My core pattern is the failed breakdown/breakout, and this comprises over 70% of my entries. Very important information on failed break down strategy found in this newsletter. Below are real time examples of the pattern from prior newsletters.

prose_context.text: 8) The core difference between amateur and professional traders is amateur trading is largely about predicting, professional trading is about reacting. My trading, and all professional trading is about reacting to price action - not predicting it. Nobody knows where price is headed, nor can anyone predict the precise pathway price will track intraday. Pro...

The failed breakout/breakdown setup is premised on the fact that the vast majority of breakouts and breakdowns are traps. Price absolutely loves to break down a support, trap those chasing, then reverse and squeeze the other way. Breakouts are therefore exceptionally hard to trade, and take an enormous amount of skill and screen time to recognize the cond...

Institutions have a set bag of tricks they use to enter the market, and the failed breakdown is #1 on that list. If you want to enter with enormous size, you need liquidity, and to find liquidity, you have to hunt for it. Fortunately for institutions, liquidity hides in very predictable spots (and it just so happens to be the spots that most retail trader...

I want to give a trade every chance to work and will never stop out arbitrarily without a support being lost, nor will I stop out due to my interpretation of price action once I am in the trade. Once I enter, I am fully committed to that trade until a stop or target hits. Every trade gets ample space and *all* the time it needs to work. 15 points is my ma...

1) Price must put in a significant low. This is the most important criteria and if this is not present, there is no Failed Breakdown. A significant low is ideally the prior days low and this is the gold standard. Absent this, a major multi-hour low can work, provided it goes 20-30+ points. Absent this, a cluster of lows can also work. It cannot be any ran...

2) The low should occur at a technically significant spot. Ideally, a major trendline or horizontal support or prior area where price broke out and is now back-testing. It can’t just be any random spot on the chart and must be in an important area that was previously significant if you look to the left of the chart.

A significant low has a precise definition and refers to one of three things: 1. The prior days low 2. A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3. A cluster or shelf of lows. We can long when the low recovers, after we see a condition called acceptance and I discuss this in enormous detail in my methodology page section 9.

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

The first low quality possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 7355. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “7345 is below there. This is also a shallow support. If its a slow grind into the zone one can buy it, but if we are free falling, don’t knife catch. A much safer entry is to wait for 7345 to defend, then recover 7355 to get long. Why 7355? At 12:30PM ...
Why was 7355 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7355 we had number #2: A clear low that was set at 12:30PM yesterday from which we rallied to the high of day ~7390.

snippet: You can see here how powerful the combination of runners and Failed Breakdowns are. Failed Breakdown of 7213 Monday, then runners did the rest. The beauty of runners is that you don’t need to know what move will go 150 points. As traders - we don’t predict.

I don’t short, I don’t chase longs, I don’t flip back and forth long and short, I don’t trade chop, I don’t trade low vol, I don’t trade because some influencer posted a trade, I don’t trade because of news. prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary

The first low quality possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 7279. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “7279 is below there. Between 11:30AM and 1:10PM today, ES set a nice multi-touch shelf of lows at 7279. This is a 5-6 touch shelf. The flush and recovery of this is actionable as a Failed Breakdown, but given its position in the trend leg from yesterda...
Why was 7279 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7279 we had number #3: A clear, multi-touch shelf of lows.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It generally involves price ...

Why was 7247 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7248 we had #1. 7248 was basically the daily low for Friday and it held for the entire session. The flush and recovery would be actionable.

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. The basic context heading into today was twofold. Firstly - at 2:58PM yesterday - ES flushed a multitouch shelf of lows at 7147 down to 7137, and recovered. 7147 was support of a consolidation or what I call a Mode 2 Range that had been in play for 48hrs 7147-7189, discussed above.

Remember Mode 2 is the default state of price action and refers to a session where price does not trend one way from open to close. Mode 2 days are rangebound sessions and about 90% of days are Mode 2. Mode 2 contrasts with Mode 1. Mode 1 days are days where price trends one way from open to close. These are very rare

Ranges like this are what I call Mode 2. Mode 2 is easy to trade as long as you follow the rules. 1) Do not predict price action. Do not predict what path price will take to fill out the range, when it will breakout, or how it will breakout. If you try to predict, you’ve lost already. Simply plan triggers, react. 2) Always manage trades level to level. In...

Around 4:00PM, earnings trapping began. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “Note we have earnings at 4pm so expect fast, wild, trappy, algo driven swings.” We trapped up to ~7189 range resistance, then trapped down to 7132 by 4:20PM. Just after that, we got the 7137 Failed Breakdown discussed above.

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

As I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “My general lean is we fill this out (perhaps trap support once more) then see 7174, 7189. Perhaps dip there, then breakout to 7204, 7220, 7238/48. Bear case discussed above.”

Around 4:00PM, earnings trapping began. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “Note we have earnings at 4pm so expect fast, wild, trappy, algo driven swings.” We trapped up to ~7189 range resistance, then trapped down to 7132 by 4:20PM. Just after that, we got the 7137 Failed Breakdown discussed above.

snippet: Ranges like this are what I call Mode 2. Mode 2 is easy to trade as long as you follow the rules. 1) Do not predict price action. Do not predict what path price will take to fill out the range, when it will breakout, or how it will breakout. If you try to predict, you’ve lost already. Simply plan triggers, react. 2) Always manage trades level to level. In...

Remember a Mode 2 range is defined as follows:

The first high quality possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 7147. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “7147 is below there. This support tested over 5x today and defended each time. The Failed Breakdown of this multi-touch shelf (all of which occurred between 830am and 1pm today) is an obvious entry. Bonus if we can tag 7136 or lower on this flush.”

Secondly, ES spent yesterday inside a tight Mode 2 range mostly 7147 to 7180. Remember Mode 2 is the default state of price action and refers to a session where price does not trend one way from open to close. Mode 2 days are rangebound sessions and about 90% of days are Mode 2. Mode 2 contrasts with Mode 1. Mode 1 days are days where price trends one way...

Mode 2 is easy to trade as long as you follow the rules. 1) Do not predict price action...
Why was 7175-80 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.
Secondly, ES spent yesterday in a tight Mode 2 range 7180-7205. I expanded in yesterday’s newsletter what a Mode 2 range means.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7175-80 we had #3 roughly: A shelf of lows. This was a 3 touch shelf referenced in the above quote.

snippet: Remember the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (7180) by 5 points (7185) and holds at or above 7185 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

prose_context.text: Remember the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (7180) by 5 points (7185) and holds at or above 7185 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

Unfortunately, we never got this last evening, and simply resumed up the targets directly. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “My general lean is always to defer to the trend. Ideally we can fill out the 7175-80 to 7200-05 range, then resume up with next target slate being 7213, 7221, then 7244.”

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

I need to see a vertical “elevator down” sell. Then I long when price flushes a low/shelf of lows for a Failed Breakdown. That is it. I don’t short, I don’t chase longs, I don’t flip back and forth long and short, I don’t trade chop, I don’t trade low vol, I don’t trade because some influencer posted a trade, I don’t trade because of news. Recall the first form price back-tests the significant low (7180) from below, sells off, then returns to it. By doing this (selling off at the significant low then rebounding back to it), price tells us there is no supply at the significant low and we can safely long above when it recovers. This was a big Failed Breakdown, and we ripped from there to 7200+ on Friday.

To summarize the trade management methodology, it therefore goes like this: Lock in 75% profits at first level up. A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7147 we had #2 roughly: A low that goes ~20+ points. At 9:40AM Friday ES set a low there and rallied to 7200.

Ranges like this are what I call Mode 2. Mode 2 is easy to trade as long as you follow the rules. 1) Do not predict price action. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

To summarize the trade management methodology, it therefore goes like this: Lock in 75% profits at first level up. From there, you can move your stop up, for me, it will usually go several points points under break-even, but I am flexible here. My only core criteria is that I *will not let the entire trade go back red*. I want to give the trade every chan...

A little after the close Tuesday, ES recovered a major low at 7097 (this low held for much of the day Tuesday), initiating a Failed Breakdown in the process. From there, we got the short squeeze to 7160+ Wednesday.

Then Wednesday evening? A little after 8pm we saw a classic Failed Breakdown. Driven by an erroneous headline, ES went elevator down a little after 8pm from 7160’s to 7105. In doing so, ES lost a major low at 7129 from Wednesday morning. Shortly after, that 7129 low recovered, and ES ripped to 7180 by noon Wednesday.

article_title: A Full Week Of Coil For SPX. Do We Trend Next Week? April 27 Pan
snippet: We saw this off the Sunday open this week. We went elevator down from 7160 and flushed to 7085 at 6pm Sunday evening. Shortly after, ES recovered a massive low set at 9:45AM on Friday at 7120 (Failed Breakdown) and rallied to 7180’s yesterday before finally going elevator down yesterday morning ultimately back to 7085 by yesterday’s close.

It traps bulls, it traps bears, and traps those looking for straight lines. This has been all week. The other 10% of...

Ranges like this are what I call Mode 2. Mode 2 is easy to trade as long as you follow the rules. 1) Do not predict price action. Do not predict what path price will take to fill out the range, when it will breakout, or how it will breakout. If you try to predict, you’ve lost already. Simply plan triggers, react. 2) Always manage trades level to level. In...

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7125 we had number 2, a multi-hour low that went 20+ points.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. snippet: The Tuesday 4:07PM Failed Breakdown of 7097

prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary
Why was 7097 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7097 we had #1: A the daily low for Tuesday. Specifically, at 1:30PM Tuesday ES bottomed there, rallied 41 points, and this acted as the low for much of the day.

Around 3:50PM yesterday, ES flushed yesterday’s 7097 low (this was a big low that held most the session) down to 7085 and recovered for a Failed Breakdown from which we ripped back up into that range.

prose_context.text: Why was 7097 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7097 we had #1: A the daily low for Tuesday. Specifically, at 1:30PM Tuesday ES bottomed there, rallied 41 points, and this acted as the low for much of the day.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It generally involves price ...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

snippet: Monday Evening and the Possible 7120 Failed Breakdown

prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary

The first possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 7120. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “Below there is 7121. There is a massive shelf of lows at 7121. Specifically at 11am we set a huge low there and rallied to 7147+. At 730AM, we set another low there and rallied 37+ points. This is a clear zone we’d want to trade the flush and recovery of and a Fai...
Why was 7120 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7120 we had #3: A shelf of lows. Specifically, at 730am and 11am yesterday ES set big lows at 7120 from which we rallied 37 and 30 points respectively. The flush and recovery here is actionable.

snippet: The 11am Elevator Down Sell into ~7120 Failed Breakdown

prose_context.text: By 11:04AM, we hit 7120 support. In the above section, I discussed the 7120 Failed Breakdown long. This one was abit awkward because we really need to get into precise levels to see the Failed Breakdown here. To be extremely precise, the 730am and 11am lows yesterday were at 7121.75 and 7121.50 respectively. Since we flushed to 7120 then recovered those 7...

At 930AM, ES put in a dip and backtested the 7147-53 resistance shelf from yesterday, and bounced 25 points. I did not long this personally (opting to just hold my runner), but I listed the support at 8am for those who wanted to engage:

Right after 11am, we recovered that shelf and I took the long here via the non-acceptance protocol, longing at 11:06AM at 7130 or so this is where we were at the time. I managed this as always: Locked in 75% profits at 7135 1st up, left 25% to run. We finally had some volatility pick up here at least and the last two weeks have offered barely any trades. ...

This one only went one level before losing that ~7121 shelf at around 1pm and we sold down to 7104. I cut my 25% runner here down to my core 10% and would be willing to add back if 7120-21 recovered again.

snippet: The first possible quality entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 7120. I wrote Friday at 4pm:

resistance_context.text: Resistances are: 7153 (major), 7159, 7169, 7174 (major), 7186, 7194 (major), 7204, 7217 (major), 7224, 7232 (major), 7239, 7245, 7253 (major), 7262, 7271, 7283 (major), 7290 (major), 7304, 7314, 7318 (major), 7330, 7341 (major), 7352, 7358 (major), 7362, 7369 (major). As readers know I don’t short ES - I only get my points on the long side, but I still gi...

prose_context.text: Why was 7120 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7120 we had #2: A multi-hour 20+ point low. At 9:45AM Friday ES put in a massive 20+ point rally from which it rallied to the high of day.

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It generally involves price ...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

The first possible entry was down was a Failed Breakdown of 7058. I wrote yesterday at 4pm: “First support as of writing though is 7058. We set two small lows there today one at 1:10PM and one at 2:20PM. Instead of buying this directly, its best to wait for that shelf of lows to flush and recover as a Failed Breakdown. 7048 is below there. This is where w...
Why was 7058 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 7058 we had #3: A shelf of lows at 7058 set at 1:10PM and 2:20PM yesterday.

Why was 6848-52 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 6848-52 we had #2: A clear cluster or shelf of lows. Specifically between 1:40PM Thursday and 12:40PM Friday ES put in a clear 5 touch shelf of lows there. The flush and recovery of this shelf would b...

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. I wrote on Friday at 4pm: “6848-52 is first support down and this has been tested 5x in the last 24hrs with one small flush today. I would not engage this zone again as it is too well tested, but if we can flush today’s 6847 low (perhaps down to 6840) and recover, this would be an action...
Why was 6848-52 flush and recovery a Failed Breakdown? Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 6848-52 we had #2: A clear cluster or shelf of lows. Specifically between 1:40PM Thursday and 12:40PM Friday ES put in a clear 5 touch shelf of lows there. The flush and recovery of this shelf would b...

As always with Failed Breakdowns though the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It generally involves price ...

prose_context.text: 1) Readers know that I talk everyday about the two siblings in ES. These siblings are elevator down sell and short squeeze, they are always paired together, and this is the tendency we exploit to generate our entries. Elevator down sell refers to a sharp flush where ES cuts through many supports with ease. This is usually how ES sells off. It rallies slow...

We saw exactly this off the Sunday open. At 6pm futures gapped down from 6615+, then went elevator down to 6565 or so. Shortly after - around 8pm - ES recovered 6593 which was a huge shelf of lows from Thursday and to a lesser extent Friday. This was a Failed Breakdown and from there, we short squeezed to 6660+ Monday high of day.

To summarize the trade management methodology, it therefore goes like this: Lock in 75% profits at first level up. From there, you can move your stop up, for me, it will usually go several points points under break-even, but I am flexible here. My only core criteria is that I *will not let the entire trade go back red*. I want to give the trade every chan...

Shortly after, we put in a Failed Breakdown of 6595 (at 11:15PM on Sunday ES put in a large bounce at 6595 to 6560s making it a clear significant low) and we ripped to 6647+ after to set the high of day for Tuesday’s regular hours. article_title: Massive Breakout Underway For SPX. How Much More Upside? April 10 Plan
snippet: We closed up yesterday around 6815 and the runner from Tuesday’s 6593 Failed Breakdown continued to work. This meant - for me - there was little to do but hold my runner and wait for the next elevator down sell.

prose_context.text: Trade Recap/Daily Summary

The first possible entry was a Level Reclaim long of 6809. I wrote yesterday at 3:30pm: “In terms of spots to add on strength, I would stick to the above entries but one possible trade is the reclaim of 6809. 6809 was a clear support/resistance shelf for the last 12hrs. Resistance from 720PM to 11pm Tuesday evening. Support from 12:20PM to 2pm Wednesday m...

This was an example of a Level Reclaim Long as opposed to a Failed Breakdown. Recall that Level Reclaims are an advanced, rarer variant of the Failed Breakdown. The Failed Breakdown is 90% of my trades and the setup I discuss above and daily. Failed Breakdowns are simple: Price sets a clear significant low or cluster of lows, sweeps it (loses it), recover...

A significant low has a precise definition and refers to one of three things: 1. The prior days low 2. A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3. A cluster or shelf of lows. We can long when the low recovers, after we see a condition called acceptance and I discuss this in enormous detail in my methodology page section 9.

article_title: Massive Breakout Underway For SPX. How Much More Upside? April 10 Plan
snippet: Remember the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (6809) by 5 points (6814) and holds at or above 6814 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

prose_context.text: Remember the non-acceptance protocol automatically activates when price recovers the significant low (6809) by 5 points (6814) and holds at or above 6814 for a few minutes. The non-acceptance protocol typically triggers for shallow (under 10 point flush of the significant low) and fast Failed Breakdowns, but you can use it for any Failed Breakdown. Typica...

Remember 5 points above the significant low is the danger zone for Failed Breakdowns and where most losses occur. If entering in this zone you need clear acceptance and if you don’t see it or it is too noisy or uncertain, just wait for the non-acceptance protocol.

As always with Failed Breakdowns OR Level Reclaims though, the golden rule is if you just rush in long on the recovery of the significant low, you lose. Prior to entry you HAVE TO see either: 1) One of the two acceptance types. Acceptance is price action that we must see prior to entry, in order to prevent us from rushing in and getting trapped. It genera...

Remember a Failed Breakdown requires price to lose and recover a significant low.
Trade Recap/Daily Summary

A significant low has three possible definitions. 1) The prior days low 2) A multi-hour low/ a low that goes 20+ points or 3) A cluster or shelf of lows. At 6598 we had #2. At 1:40PM on Tuesday, ES set a low there from which we rallied 43 points. This is a clear, V-shaped low of the sort we’d want to see a flush and recovery of.
